title: The PMO & EPMO
summary: How the project management office and its enterprise form standardise delivery and link projects to organisational strategy, viewed through PMBOK 8's Systems thinking principle and governance.

sections.heading: What a PMO is and why organisations have one
sections.body:
A Project Management Office (PMO) is an organisational structure that standardises and supports project delivery and connects that delivery to wider strategy. Rather than every project inventing its own approach, the PMO provides a common home for methods, governance, reporting and shared capability.

Organisations create a PMO to raise consistency, improve oversight across many initiatives, build delivery capability and make sure projects actually advance the goals leadership cares about. The exam frames the PMO as an enabler of value, not just an administrative function.

blocks.title: Core purpose
lines: PMO purpose = standardise delivery and link projects to strategy.
It is an enabler of value, not just administration.

blocks.title: PMBOK 8 lens
lines: The PMO supports the Systems Thinking principle: it sees projects as parts of a larger whole.
It provides governance so delivery stays aligned to organisational objectives.
It coordinates across projects, where a sponsor champions just one.

sections.heading: The three PMO types
sections.body: PMOs are classified by how much control they exert over projects. More control means more direct authority over how work is run.

rows.value: Low control. Consultative: supplies templates, training, best practices and lessons learned; acts as a repository. Teams draw on it as needed.
Moderate control. Requires compliance with a chosen framework, methodology, governance or tools, and checks that projects conform.
High control. Takes over and directly manages the projects; project managers are drawn from and report into the PMO.

blocks.title: Fast recall
lines: Supportive consults.
Controlling enforces compliance.
Directive runs the projects.

sections.heading: Core PMO functions
sections.body: Across the three types, a PMO typically delivers a recognisable set of functions.

lines: Defines the delivery approach, templates, processes and metrics.
Builds project management capability and mentors practitioners.
Tools, reporting, stage gates, assurance and oversight.
Shares scarce resources and coordinates across the portfolio.
Keeps initiatives tied to objectives and tracks expected benefits.

blocks.title: Remember as
lines: Standards - capability - governance - portfolio coordination - benefits.

sections.heading: PMO vs EPMO
sections.body:
A PMO is usually focused on delivery efficiency. It improves how projects and programmes are run and typically reports into delivery or operational leadership.

An Enterprise PMO (EPMO) sits at the enterprise level. It links the C-suite's strategy to execution, owns strategic project selection and portfolio prioritisation, and reports to top executives. The EPMO answers the question of doing the right projects, while a delivery PMO concentrates on doing projects right.

rows.value:
PMO: delivery / operational.
EPMO: enterprise / C-suite.

PMO: how projects are run.
EPMO: strategic project selection and portfolio prioritisation.

PMO: delivery or operational leadership.
EPMO: top executives.

PMO: doing projects right.
EPMO: doing the right projects.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: EPMO is strategic and reports to the top.
A PMO is delivery-focused and reports lower.

sections.heading: Project police anti-pattern and PMO value
sections.body:
A PMO that only audits and enforces, without helping teams succeed, becomes the project police. It creates friction, is resented, and is often the first thing cut because it is seen as overhead rather than value.

Effective PMOs enable and coach as well as govern, and they demonstrate measurable value such as faster delivery, fewer failed projects and tighter strategic alignment. PMO maturity grows from this basic, reactive support towards an optimised, strategic partner, and higher maturity correlates with better delivery outcomes.

groups.label: Behaviour
lines: Only audits and enforces; does not help teams succeed.

groups.label: Result
lines: Creates friction, is resented, first thing cut as overhead.

groups.label: Behaviour
lines: Enables and coaches as well as governs.

groups.label: Result
lines: Faster delivery, fewer failed projects, tighter strategic alignment.

blocks.title: Maturity
lines: Maturity grows from basic, reactive support towards an optimised, strategic partner.
Higher maturity correlates with better delivery outcomes.

blocks.title: Best answer
lines: Pick the PMO that enables and proves value, not one that just polices compliance.
PMO aligns standards across many projects; a sponsor accepts and champions one project.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded and reorganised for a PMBOK 8 principle-based PMP exam.
